metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cardinality assertion inside `test_enrich_quality` (e.g. `assert len(fixtures) >= 6, f'expected >=6 quality fixtures, got {len(fixtures)}'`) and/or a standalone `test_fixture_count` so the proving command fails when fewer than 6 fixtures are committed.
findings.finding: `test_enrich_quality` iterates over fixture files and asserts gold membership per fixture, but no test or proving command asserts that at least 6 fixture files are actually present. If the `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/` directory is empty or missing, the test loop runs zero iterations and passes vacuously — the '>=6 committed fixtures' AC is then trivially satisfied with no real coverage. This is a verify-command defect: missing cardinality assertion (taxonomy defect #5).
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of `test_enrich_quality`, add: `assert len(fixture_files) >= 6, f'Need >=6 quality fixtures, found {len(fixture_files)}'` where `fixture_files = list(FIXTURE_DIR.glob('*.json'))`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Testing section how `test_enrich_quality` supplies the fixture `body` to `enrich()` — e.g. add a `body=` override kwarg to the op signature (and note it in 'Scope'), or document that the test patches `rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text` to return `fixture['body']`.
findings.finding: The quality fixture test is described as calling `enrich(...)` 'on the fixture body', but the op signature `enrich(ticket_id, *, repo_root=None, config=None, runner=None)` has no `body=` parameter and internally calls `_ticket_text(ticket)` to fetch the ticket's text. The plan never specifies how the unit test supplies the fixture's `body` text without a real ticket or a mock of `_ticket_text`.
findings.location: Testing section ('calls enrich(...) on the fixture body') vs 'What' section (op signature and internal use of `_ticket_text`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `test_enrich_quality` feeds the fixture `body` to `enrich()` without a live ticket store — either add a `body=` injection parameter to `enrich()` that bypasses the store fetch, specify that the test seeds an in-process ticket store with the fixture body, or change the fixture shape to use a pre-seeded ticket_id from a test-time store.
findings.finding: The `test_enrich_quality` fixture mechanism is broken: fixtures carry `body: str` but `enrich(ticket_id, ...)` fetches ticket text from the store via `_ticket_text(state)` (which requires a real ticket state dict obtained from `show_state`). The plan never specifies how the offline quality test resolves this — either `enrich` needs a body-injection escape hatch, the test needs a seeded in-process ticket store, or the fixture needs a different shape. As written, calling `enrich(fixture_ticket_id, runner=FakeRunner(...))` in the offline test will attempt a real `show_state` call against a non-existent ticket, failing before the FakeRunner even runs.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_enrich_quality` fixture design; fixture shape `{'ticket_id': str, 'body': str, ...}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `test_enrich_quality` supplies fixture `body` text to `enrich()` without a live ticket lookup — either add a `body=` / `ticket_text=` override parameter to the signature, document a mock/patch for `_ticket_text`, or revise the test description to name the injection mechanism explicitly.
findings.finding: The quality-fixture test is described as calling `enrich(...)` 'on the fixture body', but the only specified signature is `enrich(ticket_id, *, repo_root=None, config=None, runner=None)` — there is no `body=` or text-injection parameter. The plan does not specify how the test supplies fixture body text to `enrich()` without a live ticket-lookup (no mock of `_ticket_text` or ticket fetch is described).
findings.location: Testing section — `test_enrich_quality` description; Scope section — `enrich.py` signature; `## What` section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `ticket_text: str | None = None` parameter to `enrich()`: when provided, skip `_ticket_text(ticket)` and use it directly as `instructions`. This makes offline testing trivial, matches the fixture schema (`body` field), and is invisible to production callers (who always pass `ticket_id` only).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the prompt file name and the `get_prompt` id: either rename the file to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket-digest.md` (so the auto-derived id matches `'ticket-digest'`), or keep `enrich.md` and change every `get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)` call to `get_prompt('enrich', ...)`, updating the Scope section and AC accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously names the prompt file `reviewers/enrich.md` (stem='enrich') AND calls `get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)`, but `_packaged_prompt_files()` derives the prompt id directly from the file stem: 'enrich.md' → id 'enrich'. For `get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)` to resolve the packaged prompt, the file must be named `ticket-digest.md`. As specified, the op's `get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)` call will raise `PromptNotFound` against the 'enrich.md' file.
findings.location: ## What — system_prompt assembly paragraph; Scope — 'Prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the prompt file to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket-digest.md` — the id `'ticket-digest'` is the more semantically clear name, matching the schema constant `TICKET_DIGEST = "ticket_digest"`, and the auto-derivation in `_packaged_prompt_files()` will yield `'ticket-digest'` from that stem.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the prompt file name with the prompt id used in `get_prompt()`: either rename `reviewers/enrich.md` → `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (resolves to id `'ticket-digest'`) or change the `get_prompt` call-site to use `'enrich'` (matching the file name `enrich.md`).
findings.finding: The prompt file is named `reviewers/enrich.md` but the op calls `prompts.get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)`. The `_packaged_prompt_files()` function maps a packaged `*.md` file to its prompt id by normalizing the file stem: `enrich.md` → id `'enrich'`, NOT `'ticket-digest'`. For `get_prompt('ticket-digest')` to succeed, the file must be named `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (→ id `'ticket-digest'`) or `reviewers/ticket-digest.md`. As specified, `get_prompt('ticket-digest')` will raise `PromptNotFound` at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the file to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket_digest.md` — this produces prompt id `'ticket-digest'` (via `ticket_digest`.replace(`_`, `-`)) and matches the call-site `get_prompt('ticket-digest', ...)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the prompt file name and the `get_prompt()` call id: either rename the prompt file to `ticket-digest.md` (so `_packaged_prompt_files()` maps it to id `"ticket-digest"`) or change the `get_prompt()` call to use `"enrich"` (matching the file stem of `enrich.md`).
findings.finding: The prompt id passed to `get_prompt()` in the op (`"ticket-digest"`) does not match the prompt id that `_packaged_prompt_files()` will derive from the specified file name `enrich.md` (stem `enrich` → id `"enrich"`). At runtime `get_prompt("ticket-digest")` raises `PromptNotFound` because no file named `ticket-digest.md` is on disk.
findings.location: What section / System prompt + instructions assembly paragraph: `prompts.get_prompt("ticket-digest", repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` vs. Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the prompt file to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket-digest.md` (the `_packaged_prompt_files()` resolver strips `.md` and maps `ticket-digest` → id `"ticket-digest"`, matching the `get_prompt("ticket-digest", ...)` call). Alternatively, keep `enrich.md` and change the op call to `get_prompt("enrich", ...)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding assertion in `test_prompt_excludes_logs` or a new `test_prompt_front_matter`) that statically asserts the `enrich.md` front-matter contains `outputs: ticket_digest`, does NOT contain `category: review`, and contains `execution_mode: single_turn`.
findings.finding: No AC verifies that the prompt's front-matter constraints are correct — specifically `NO category: review` (which prevents `reviewers/index.json` from indexing `enrich.md` as a reviewer) and `outputs: ticket_digest` (which wires the schema). `test_prompt_excludes_logs` only checks for the log-exclusion directive, not front-matter fields. A mis-categorized prompt would silently inject the enrich prompt into reviewer selection.
findings.location: AC section (only `test_prompt_excludes_logs` covers the prompt) vs Scope section ('NO category: review' constraint on enrich.md front-matter)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_prompt_excludes_logs` (or add a sibling `test_prompt_front_matter`) to parse the YAML front-matter of `reviewers/enrich.md` and assert: `'category' not in front_matter`, `front_matter['outputs'] == 'ticket_digest'`, and `front_matter['execution_mode'] == 'single_turn'`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `ticket_digest_response_model()` from the `enrich.py` Scope bullet and state only `contracts.py` as the authoritative location, so the two sections agree.
findings.finding: The Scope section says `ticket_digest_response_model()` is defined in `enrich.py` ('New op module … defining `enrich(ticket_id, ...)` and the zero-arg pydantic builder `ticket_digest_response_model()`'), while the contract registration section says to define it 'inside `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py`'. An executor following Scope puts it in `enrich.py`; following the contract-registration section puts it in `contracts.py`. These are mutually exclusive locations.
findings.location: Scope bullet for `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py`; 'Contract registration + bootstrap' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the `enrich.py` scope bullet to: 'New op module `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` defining `enrich(ticket_id, ...)` — the builder `ticket_digest_response_model()` lives in `contracts.py` (see below).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the phrase 'and the zero-arg pydantic builder `ticket_digest_response_model()`' from the 'What' section's `enrich.py` bullet so only the 'Scope' section (contracts.py) is authoritative for that artifact's location.
findings.finding: The 'What' section names `enrich.py` as the home of the zero-arg builder `ticket_digest_response_model()`, but the 'Scope' section explicitly mandates it lives in `contracts.py` to avoid import cycles. An implementer reading top-to-bottom receives two conflicting locations for the same artifact.
findings.location: 'What' section (paragraph 'New op module src/rebar/llm/enrich.py defining ... the zero-arg pydantic builder ticket_digest_response_model()') vs 'Scope' section ('define the builder ticket_digest_response_model() inside src/rebar/llm/contracts.py')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, change 'defining enrich(ticket_id, ...) and the zero-arg pydantic builder ticket_digest_response_model()' to 'defining enrich(ticket_id, ...)' — the builder's location is fully specified in the 'Scope' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `enrich()` resolves `ticket_id` to a ticket object (e.g. state that `body: str | None = None` is an override parameter, or name the fetch function), and update the quality-fixture test description to show exactly which argument carries the fixture `body` string.
findings.finding: The plan says `enrich(ticket_id, ...)` takes a ticket_id string, but the canonical text assembler used is `_ticket_text(ticket)` which requires a ticket *object*. The plan never specifies how `ticket_id` is resolved to a ticket object (e.g. DB lookup, in-memory registry, or a caller-supplied `body=` override parameter). This is compounded by the quality fixture test, which describes calling `enrich(...)` 'on the fixture body' while the function signature accepts only `ticket_id` — an executor cannot determine whether to add a `body=` parameter, mock the ticket-fetch, or create a ticket stub.
findings.location: Scope → `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` signature; Testing → `test_enrich_quality`; 'What' section — 'assembled via the canonical normalizer … _ticket_text(ticket)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `body: str | None = None` keyword argument to `enrich()`: when supplied it is used directly as the instructions string (bypassing `_ticket_text`), enabling both the quality fixtures (pass `body=fixture["body"]`) and future callers. State this explicitly in the Scope and Testing sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the ticket-fetch step explicitly in the `enrich` op data-flow: which function is called to fetch the compiled state dict (e.g., `show_state(ticket_id, cfg.tickets_path or cfg.repo_path)`), how `tracker` is resolved from the op's config, and whether `ReadError` is re-raised as-is or wrapped.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `enrich(ticket_id)` obtains the ticket state and tracker path needed to call `_ticket_text(state)`. `_ticket_text` takes a compiled state dict (from `show_state(ticket_id, tracker)`), but the op's signature is `enrich(ticket_id, *, repo_root=None, config=None, runner=None)`. The `tracker` argument (the `.tickets-tracker/` directory path) is not mentioned, and neither is the `show_state` call. The plan is silent on how the op resolves the ticket store path from `repo_root` / `config`.
findings.location: What section — 'instructions are the ticket's canonical text — assembled via the canonical normalizer rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text(ticket)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'fetch the compiled ticket state: `state = show_state(ticket_id, cfg.tickets_path or cfg.repo_path)` (re-raises `ReadError` on a missing ticket); then assemble instructions via `_ticket_text(state)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify or restructure test_enrich_quality so it does not tautologically assert gold membership on a FakeRunner-seeded fixture digest: either (a) rename it to test_enrich_passthrough and make its intent explicit (it tests that enrich() does not corrupt fields, not extraction quality), or (b) replace the gold-membership assertion with a structural assertion (all four fields present, propositions is a non-empty list of strings) that is distinct from what test_schema_valid already covers, and move the quality signal exclusively to test_enrich_quality_live.
findings.finding: test_enrich_quality is a tautological/self-authored-oracle test: it seeds FakeRunner with fixture["digest"] (a captured real model output), calls enrich() which passes those fields through, and asserts gold strings appear in the result. Since the gold strings are pre-verified to be in the fixture at capture time, the test can only fail if enrich() corrupts/drops fields — which is already covered by test_no_nondeterministic_fields. The test adds no signal about whether the prompt or extraction logic produces correct digests; it locks in whatever the model happened to output during fixture generation and would pass silently after a prompt regression (as long as fixtures aren't regenerated).
findings.suggested_fix: Rename test_enrich_quality to test_enrich_op_path to signal its true scope (op assembly + field pass-through), replace the gold-membership assertion with a structural/schema assertion not duplicated by test_schema_valid (e.g. assert all four fields are non-empty), and add a comment noting that extraction quality is validated only by the live-gated test_enrich_quality_live. This eliminates the tautology and makes the test suite's intent honest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_propositions_min/max` need to be full `LLMConfig` fields+env-vars (serving only `enrich`), or whether module-level constants in `enrich.py` (e.g. `_MIN_PROPOSITIONS = 2`, `_MAX_PROPOSITIONS = 6`) suffice — the plan cites no AC or consumer story that requires operator tunability of these bounds.
findings.finding: Two new `LLMConfig` dataclass fields (`overlap_propositions_min`, `overlap_propositions_max`) + two env vars + two `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys are added via the full `_llm_int` resolution machinery, but these bounds have exactly one consumer (`enrich`) and no AC or sibling-story done-definition requires them to be operator-tunable. The existing `_llm_int` knobs (max_tokens, max_iterations, timeout_s) serve every op; these serve only `enrich`. The post-processing logic (truncate/flag) is purely internal to `enrich` and its 2/6 defaults are already prompt-encoded—making them config-file+env tunables is premature config-surface proliferation with no demonstrated need.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/config.py` edit target; 'Config-bound threading' section
findings.suggested_fix: Define `_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN = 2` and `_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MAX = 6` as private module constants in `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py`. Remove the two `LLMConfig` fields and `_llm_int` lines. If operator tunability is genuinely needed later (when a second consumer exists), promote them to config then.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either expose `_ticket_text` as a public API in `rebar._engine_support.gates` (rename to `ticket_text` and add to `__all__`), or note the private-symbol coupling explicitly in the Dependencies section and add `rebar._engine_support.gates` to the Scope edit targets.
findings.finding: The plan has `enrich.py` (in `src/rebar/llm/`) import and call `rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text` — a leading-underscore private function in a different top-level subpackage (`rebar._engine_support`). This coupling is not enumerated as a consumer/dependency in the Scope or Dependencies sections. A rename or relocation of `_ticket_text` silently breaks `enrich.py` without any import error at module load time (it only fails at call time).
findings.location: What section — instructions assembly paragraph; Scope section (`src/rebar/llm/enrich.py`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CLAUDE.md entry or ADR capturing the Cupid/LLM-feature-extractor decision (why over embeddings, the recall@20 experiment result, and the digest schema rationale) so the architectural choice is durable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — using an LLM as a one-time feature extractor (Cupid/arXiv:2308.10022 pattern) as the load-bearing dedup artifact — but specifies no update to CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or any other durable design document. Future maintainers will have no durable record of why this pattern was chosen over embeddings or other approaches.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single `docs/adr/NNNN-cupid-enrichment-op.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section under `rebar.llm`) recording: (1) the Cupid pattern citation, (2) the recall@20=1.0 experiment result as the justification, (3) the digest schema field rationale, and (4) why embeddings/heavy ML were excluded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the error contract whether the framework runner handles retry/backoff for transient Anthropic errors (rate limits, timeouts), or whether `enrich()` / its callers must; if the runner does NOT, specify a retry policy or at minimum document the expected caller behavior.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the error contract for structural failures (`FindingsError`, `StructuredOutputError`) and configuration failures (`LLMUnavailableError`) but says nothing about resilience against transient LLM failures — rate-limit errors, network timeouts, or 5xx responses from the Anthropic API. It is not stated whether the framework runner handles retries or whether callers (S1/S2/S7) are expected to retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability note to `enrich.py` specifying at minimum a structured log entry on `FindingsError`/`StructuredOutputError` (ticket_id, error type, trace_id if available) so failures are visible to operators.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention any observability instrumentation (log statements, metrics, or trace emission) for the enrichment call beyond the `trace_id` already embedded in the run result. New LLM failure points in production should be observable so operators can detect enrichment outages or elevated error rates.
findings.location: ## Error contract / ## Return shape & processing order
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich()`, wrap the runner call in a try/except that logs `{ticket_id, error_type, trace_id}` at WARNING level before re-raising; this satisfies the 'surfaced, not swallowed' reliability check without introducing new infrastructure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Link or inline-quote the epic experiment comment (ticket ID + relevant excerpt) and confirm the experiment used the same four-field schema and proposition bounds specified in this plan — the recall@20=1.0 claim is the feature's primary justification and must be auditable.
findings.finding: The recall experiment result ('digest-BM25F retrieves 8/8 heavy paraphrases at rank 1, recall@20 = 1.0') is cited as an 'epic experiment comment' — an internal artifact that is not linked, quoted, or reproducible from the plan. This is a load-bearing capability assertion: the entire feature's justification rests on it, yet no reviewer can verify the experimental setup, the corpus size, or whether BM25F is actually the retrieval mechanism being implemented here.
findings.location: ## Why — 'DERISKED: the recall experiment (see epic experiment comment) showed digest-BM25F retrieves 8/8 heavy paraphrases at rank 1 (recall@20 = 1.0)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Experiment reference' subsection under ## Why with: the epic/ticket ID, the paraphrase corpus description (N tickets, how paraphrases were constructed), the exact schema used, and the BM25F index configuration — or quote the key result inline if the full artifact is in the epic.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
